description:
Your goal is to prepare a small “sandbox” for a web-developer and to document the
        permissions you create.

        What you must accomplish (roughly 5–10 shell commands are sufficient):

        1.  Create a directory named “webapp” directly inside /home/user.  
        2.  Inside that directory create the following sub-directories EXACTLY as named:  
                • public  
                • config  
                • scripts  
        3.  Place one configuration file inside the config directory called “settings.json”
            containing the following **exact JSON** (make sure the file ends with a
            trailing newline):

            {
              "env": "development",
              "debug": true
            }

        4.  Place one shell script inside the scripts directory called “backup.sh”
            with this **exact three-line content** (also ending with a trailing newline):

            #!/usr/bin/env bash
            # Simple backup script
            tar -czf /home/user/webapp_backup_$(date +%F).tar.gz /home/user/webapp

        5.  Apply these octal permissions (use chmod) so that the automated grader can
            check them without resolving symbolic strings:

                /home/user/webapp                         → 755  
                /home/user/webapp/public                  → 755  
                /home/user/webapp/config                  → 750  
                /home/user/webapp/config/settings.json    → 640  
                /home/user/webapp/scripts                 → 750  
                /home/user/webapp/scripts/backup.sh       → 700  

            (Make sure “backup.sh” keeps its executable bit.)

        6.  Create a plain-text log file called
            /home/user/permission_report.txt that lists the final permissions you set.
            The file MUST contain exactly six lines, one path per line, in the precise
            order shown below and **nothing else** (no extra spaces, no comments):

            /home/user/webapp 755  
            /home/user/webapp/public 755  
            /home/user/webapp/config 750  
            /home/user/webapp/config/settings.json 640  
            /home/user/webapp/scripts 750  
            /home/user/webapp/scripts/backup.sh 700  

        The grader will validate:

            • existence of every directory and file  
            • the exact octal permission bits  
            • the exact contents of settings.json and backup.sh (including newlines)  
            • the exact six-line permission_report.txt, byte for byte  

        You do NOT need root privileges; all the work happens inside /home/user.

        Hints (do NOT print commands here):  
            • use mkdir -p for nested folders  
            • chmod with octal notation (e.g., 755)  
            • echo or cat EOF blocks to create files with exact content  
            • test your permission log with “cat -A” to ensure no stray whitespace
truth:
Expected directory structure and permissions after successful completion:

        1. Directories
           /home/user/webapp                      (mode 0755)
           /home/user/webapp/public               (mode 0755)
           /home/user/webapp/config               (mode 0750)
           /home/user/webapp/scripts              (mode 0750)

        2. Files
           /home/user/webapp/config/settings.json (mode 0640)
           /home/user/webapp/scripts/backup.sh    (mode 0700)
           /home/user/permission_report.txt       (mode 0664 or stricter; content verified)

        Content of /home/user/webapp/config/settings.json
        -------------------------------------------------
        {
          "env": "development",
          "debug": true
        }
        (file ends with single \n)

        Content of /home/user/webapp/scripts/backup.sh
        ----------------------------------------------
        #!/usr/bin/env bash
        # Simple backup script
        tar -czf /home/user/webapp_backup_$(date +%F).tar.gz /home/user/webapp
        (file ends with single \n)

        Content of /home/user/permission_report.txt
        -------------------------------------------
        /home/user/webapp 755
        /home/user/webapp/public 755
        /home/user/webapp/config 750
        /home/user/webapp/config/settings.json 640
        /home/user/webapp/scripts 750
        /home/user/webapp/scripts/backup.sh 700
        (file ends with single \n)

        The autograder will:
          • stat the paths for mode verification,
          • md5sum settings.json, backup.sh, and permission_report.txt for exact-content match.
